questions.question: What is the fundamental difference between a message queue (SQS) and a message stream (Kinesis)?
options: In a queue, a message is consumed and deleted by one consumer; in a stream, the record persists and multiple independent consumer groups can read it at their own position.
A queue guarantees strict ordering of all messages while a stream delivers messages in random order to improve throughput.
SQS supports fan-out to multiple subscribers while Kinesis only allows a single consumer per stream.
Streams are used for commands (do something) and queues are used for events (something happened).
questions.explanation: The defining difference is consumption semantics. SQS removes a message once a consumer successfully deletes it — no other consumer sees it. Kinesis retains records for the configured retention period (24h–365d) and each consumer group tracks its own read position independently, enabling replay and multiple simultaneous consumers.

questions.question: Why must every SQS production queue have a Dead Letter Queue (DLQ) configured?
options: Without a DLQ, a malformed message that causes the consumer to fail on every attempt will be retried indefinitely, blocking the queue and preventing all subsequent valid messages from being processed.
AWS requires a DLQ on all SQS queues for compliance; queues without a DLQ are automatically deleted after 24 hours.
A DLQ increases SQS throughput by offloading failed messages to a separate queue and freeing the primary queue for new writes.
SQS only guarantees at-least-once delivery when a DLQ is configured; without one, messages may be silently dropped.
questions.explanation: A poison pill message — one that reliably causes consumer failure — will be retried up to maxReceiveCount times and then dropped unless a DLQ is configured. Without a DLQ, the message is deleted after max retries with no record. With a DLQ, the message is preserved for inspection and replay. More importantly, the DLQ alarm gives you immediate visibility when something is broken.

questions.question: You need to broadcast an order.placed event to 8 downstream services, each processing independently at their own pace. Which architecture is correct?
options: SNS topic with 8 SQS queue subscriptions — each service reads from its own queue, providing fan-out with per-consumer buffering and backpressure isolation.
8 separate SQS queues written to directly by the order service — one write per event multiplied by the number of consumers.
A single SQS Standard queue shared by all 8 consumers, relying on SQS visibility timeout to ensure each service processes every message.
Direct Lambda invocations from the order service to each of the 8 downstream service handlers in parallel.
questions.explanation: SNS → SQS fan-out is the canonical AWS pattern. The order service publishes once to SNS. SNS delivers to all 8 SQS queues simultaneously. Each service consumes from its dedicated queue at its own rate. Backpressure is isolated — a slow analytics consumer does not affect the fast fulfillment consumer. Direct writes to 8 queues couple the order service to every downstream service.

questions.question: When should you choose EventBridge over SNS for cross-service event routing? (Trade-off question)
options: Use EventBridge when consumers only need a subset of event types — EventBridge rules filter events server-side before delivery, avoiding unnecessary compute in consumers that would otherwise receive and discard irrelevant events.
Use EventBridge when you need higher throughput than SNS, because EventBridge handles 10x more events per second than an SNS topic.
Use EventBridge instead of SNS whenever the event payload exceeds 256 KB, because SNS has a strict 256 KB message size limit.
EventBridge should replace SNS for all use cases because SNS is deprecated in favor of EventBridge in all new AWS architectures.
questions.explanation: EventBridge adds content-based filtering: a rule evaluates each event's payload and delivers it only to targets whose filter pattern matches. This saves downstream compute and reduces coupling — the fulfillment service does not receive order.cancelled events it would have to discard. SNS has filter policies but they are coarser; EventBridge also adds a schema registry and cross-account routing that SNS lacks.

questions.question: Why use Step Functions Standard workflow instead of a Lambda chain for Amazon's 7-step order fulfillment pipeline? (Failure mode question)
options: If step 4 of 7 fails in a Lambda chain, there is no durable record of which steps succeeded — the entire pipeline must restart from step 1. Step Functions stores state durably between steps and resumes from the failed step after retry.
Lambda chains cannot call DynamoDB or SQS, so Step Functions is required to integrate with other AWS services in a multi-step workflow.
Lambda functions have a maximum timeout of 15 seconds while a Step Functions state can wait indefinitely, making Lambda chains impossible for workflows longer than 15 seconds.
Step Functions is cheaper than Lambda for workflows with 7 steps because Step Functions does not charge for compute time, only for state transitions.
questions.explanation: Lambda chains are stateless. A crash at step 4 leaves no trace of steps 1–3. You either replay the whole pipeline (risking duplicate charges) or lose the order. Step Functions writes durable execution history before and after each state. A failure at step 4 is recoverable: you see exactly which step failed, what the input was, and you can retry from that step without re-executing earlier ones.

questions.question: SQS FIFO queues guarantee ordering within a message group and support deduplication. What is their throughput ceiling and why does this matter for Prime Day?
options: 300 TPS per queue (3,000 TPS with high-throughput mode). At Prime Day's 100K orders/min (~1,667 TPS), a single FIFO queue would be saturated — you must shard across multiple queues by order ID prefix to distribute load while preserving per-order ordering.
10,000 TPS per queue, which is sufficient for Prime Day peak without any sharding.
FIFO queues have no throughput limit because AWS dynamically adds queue capacity as load increases.
300 messages per second, but this limit applies only to the deduplication check — the queue can otherwise process unlimited messages per second.
questions.explanation: SQS FIFO is hard-limited at 300 TPS (standard) or 3,000 TPS (high-throughput mode). Prime Day's 100K orders/min is ~1,667 TPS — exceeding the standard limit. Sharding by order ID prefix (e.g., first hex character of UUID → 16 queues) distributes load while keeping all events for one order in the same queue, preserving per-order ordering.

questions.question: A new regulatory requirement needs 7 days of order event replay for compliance audits. Which service supports this and why can SQS not serve this requirement?
options: Kinesis Data Streams with retention extended to 7 days. SQS has a maximum retention of 14 days but deletes messages once consumed — replaying to a new consumer is impossible because the messages no longer exist.
SQS Standard with a 7-day visibility timeout set on every message, preventing deletion until the audit consumer has read each message.
SNS with message archiving enabled, which stores all published messages for up to 30 days for replay.
EventBridge with an event archive configured, which can replay any event to any rule from the last 7 days.
questions.explanation: SQS messages are deleted after successful consumption. A second consumer cannot re-read an already-consumed SQS message. Kinesis retains records at the configured retention period regardless of consumer reads; each consumer group tracks its own iterator position independently. Note: EventBridge archives are also a valid AWS option for replay, but Kinesis is the more scalable choice for high-volume event streams.

questions.question: When should you use Step Functions Express workflow instead of Standard workflow?
options: Use Express for high-volume short-lived workflows (under 5 minutes, thousands per second) where at-least-once execution semantics are acceptable — Express is ~40x cheaper per transition than Standard but does not guarantee exactly-once state execution.
Use Express whenever the workflow has more than 10 steps, because Standard workflow has a maximum of 10 state transitions per execution.
Use Express for workflows that require human approval steps, because Standard does not support .waitForTaskToken callback patterns.
Express should always be preferred because it offers the same guarantees as Standard at lower cost, with no trade-offs.
questions.explanation: Express workflows cost $1.00/million transitions vs Standard's $25.00/million. For high-volume, short-lived automations (e.g., per-click event processing, micro-batch transforms) Express is far cheaper. The trade-off: Express is at-least-once (a step may execute more than once on retry) and the audit trail lives only in CloudWatch Logs. Standard is exactly-once per state with full console history — required for financial workflows like order fulfillment.
